primary_diagnosis.name: Acute sacroiliitis / inflammatory back-pain flare in non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis

pathophysiology_summary: Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (HLA-B27, normal plain radiographs) causes inflammatory back pain and sacroiliitis with the hallmark pattern of pain worse with rest/immobility and improved by movement, plus prolonged morning stiffness. A prolonged sitting period triggered a severe paraspinal/sacroiliac spasm, which the patient fears is a ruptured disc or spinal cord injury. The critical exclusion is cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, bilateral leg weakness) and severe radiculopathy — both screenable by history over telehealth. With those absent and an inflammatory pattern, management is anti-inflammatory therapy, frequent gentle movement (rest worsens inflammatory back pain) and heat, with a firm cauda equina safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Rest-worsened, movement-eased low-back/SI spasm on known HLA-B27 axSpA.
Severe back pain mandates excluding saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction and leg weakness.

differential.diagnosis: Acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: No shooting leg pain/dermatomal loss; SLR negative for radicular pain.
No trauma, no midline point tenderness, young with normal bone profile.
No cauda equina features (DI-001), no severe radiculopathy (DI-002), inflammatory pattern (rest-worse, movement-better) on known axSpA (DI-003) — an inflammatory flare, not cord/nerve compression.
No saddle anaesthesia/bladder-bowel dysfunction/leg weakness — cauda equina not supported (safety-net retained).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: NSAIDs, frequent gentle movement (not bed rest), heat, routine GP/rheumatology follow-up, and a firm cauda equina safety-net. Reassuring inflammatory back pain WITHOUT the cauda equina screen, or over-triaging to ED without red flags, are both errors.

red_flags.description: Cauda equina syndrome — saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, or bilateral leg weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical emergency; delayed decompression causes permanent deficit.

red_flags.description: Severe radiculopathy — progressive leg weakness or dense dermatomal numbness

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring severe back pain without explicitly screening saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel and leg weakness.
Recommending complete rest for inflammatory back pain, which worsens it — frequent gentle movement is correct.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal neurological examination (power, reflexes, sensation, SLR)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: NSAIDs + frequent gentle movement + heat + routine follow-up, with a firm cauda equina safety-net; emergency escalation for cauda equina/severe radiculopathy features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without the cauda equina screen could miss a cord/root emergency; over-triaging inflammatory back pain wastes resources.
